--- SOURCE 1: [PDF] Digital wellness (also known as digital wellbeing or digital health) ---
URL: https://htu.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/WellU-Weekly-Wellness-Newsletter-Volume-3-Issue-4.pdf

SUMMARY:
This involves cultivating healthy digital habits, such as limiting screen time, practicing digital detoxes, and regularly auditing and curating

FULL CONTENT:
Digital wellness (also known as digital wellbeing or digital health) refers to one’s intentional pursuit of creating a healthy balance (or relationship) with technology, both in the workplace and in our personal lives (Citrix, 2024; Thomas et al., 2022). Simply, digital wellness encompasses the balance and healthy usage of digital technologies to enhance overall well-being.
In today's fast-paced digital age, where technology permeates nearly every aspect of our lives, the concept of digital wellness has become increasingly relevant. Excessive screen time, digital addiction, cyberbullying, and online privacy concerns are among the challenges that underscore the importance of prioritizing digital wellness (Thomas et al., 2022; Vanden Abeele et al., 2022).
By adopting mindful and intentional approaches to digital consumption, individuals can cultivate healthier relationships with technology. In this digital era, fostering digital wellness is not just a choice but a necessity for holistic well-being. On this issue of our wellness newsletter, we dive into digital wellness and share strategies for a good digital wellbeing.
Carlos M. Cervantes, PhD, CAPE Activity Director WellU RAM Wellness Program What is digital wellness and why is it important?
In today's technology dependent world, the concept of digital wellness has emerged as a crucial aspect of overall well-being. Digital wellness refers to the mindful and intentional use of digital technologies to promote physical, mental, and emotional health (Thakral, 2023). As our reliance on digital devices continues to grow, it becomes increasingly important to understand the impact of our digital habits on our lives.
Digital wellness intertwines with various other dimensions of wellness, including: Physical Wellness. Excessive screen time and sedentary behavior can lead to health issues such as eye strain, disrupted sleep patterns, and musculoskeletal problems. Prioritizing regular breaks from screens, maintaining good posture, and engaging in physical activity can help mitigate these risks (Zeeni et al., 2018; Subrahmanyam & Smahel, 2010).
Emotional Wellness: Constant exposure to digital notifications, social media, and online content can contribute to stress, anxiety, and feelings of overwhelm. Moreover, social media platforms often facilitate comparison, cyberbullying, and negative self-talk, impacting emotional well-being. Fostering healthy relationships, seeking support when needed, and curating a positive online environment can help maintain emotional balance. Practicing digital mindfulness, setting boundaries around device usage, and cultivating offline hobbies and interests are essential for protecting mental health (Boumosleh & Jaalouk, 2017; Abi-Jaoude, Naylor & Pignatiello, 2020; Pera, 2020).
Social Wellness. Excessive digital use can interfere with face-to-face interactions and deepen feelings of isolation. Nurturing meaningful connections offline, prioritizing quality time with loved ones, and setting aside device-free zones can strengthen relationships and foster a sense of belonging (Twenge, 2017; Sha, Sariyska, Riedle, Lachmann & Montag, 2018).
Intellectual and Occupational Wellness. Constant multitasking and digital distractions can impair concentration and productivity. Implementing strategies such as time management techniques, digital detoxes, and minimizing notifications can enhance focus and efficiency.
In conclusion, digital wellness is essential for navigating the digital age mindfully and responsibly.
By cultivating healthy digital habits, setting boundaries, and prioritizing self-care, individuals can harness the benefits of technology while safeguarding their physical, mental, and emotional well-being. As we strive for a balanced and harmonious relationship with technology, prioritizing digital wellness becomes paramount in leading fulfilling and meaningful lives in the digital age.
There is no question that technology is a fundamental part of everyone’s life. In fact, data from the Pew Research Center indicate that 95% of U.S. adults report using the internet. Furthermore, they also reported that 97% of US adults (18 and older) own a smartphone and 68% of Americans report using some type of social media (including Instagram, YouTube, Snapchat, TikTok, X, or LinkedIn) (Pew Research Center, 2024).
Given the pervasiveness of technology and increased access to social media through smartphones, researchers from a variety of academic fields are studying the impact of social media on our health and well-being. The research highlights serious concerns about the contribution of social media to higher rates of depression and anxiety and lower rates of self-esteem among young adults and adolescents (Abi-Jaoude, Naylor & Pignatiello, 2020; Pera, 2020). In an era where digital devices dominate our daily routines, it's increasingly vital to recognize the value of disconnecting from social media and reconnecting with the real world.
While social media platforms offer connectivity and entertainment, excessive usage can have detrimental effects on our mental, emotional, and physical well-being (Beyari, 2023; Meier & Reinecke, 2021).
Spending less time on social media and more time engaging in face-to-face interactions and outdoor activities can lead to numerous benefits. Here's why: Enhanced Mental Health. Recent research findings have established a correlation between excessive social media use and increased rates of anxiety, depression, and feelings of loneliness. By stepping away from the screen and fostering meaningful offline connections, individuals can experience improved mood and mental resilience (Riehm et al., 2019; Beyari, 2023).
Authentic Connections. While social media allows us to stay connected with friends and family, it often fosters shallow interactions and comparisons. Investing time in real-world relationships enables deeper connections and fosters a sense of belonging and community (Karim et al., 2020) Outdoor's Therapeutic Effects. Spending time outdoors has been linked to reduced stress levels, improved mood, and enhanced creativity. Whether it's a leisurely stroll in the park, a hike in the mountains, or a day at the beach, immersing oneself in nature offers a rejuvenating escape from the digital world (Bunch, 2023).
Physical Well-Being. Excessive screen time is associated with sedentary behavior and a lack of physical activity, which can contribute to health issues such as obesity and cardiovascular problems (Duncan et al., 2012; Boone et al., 2007). By prioritizing outdoor pursuits like walking, cycling, or playing sports, individuals can promote their physical fitness and overall health.
Mindful Living. Disconnecting from social media allows individuals to be more present and mindful in their daily lives. Instead of constantly seeking validation or distractions online, embracing moments of stillness and solitude can foster self-awareness and inner peace (Klase, Connors & Abhari, 2022).
In conclusion, while social media undoubtedly has its benefits, it is important to create a balance while prioritizing real-world experiences; that is, strengthening in-person human connections. By spending less time on social media and more time engaging in social interactions and outdoor activities, individuals can nurture their well-being and lead more fulfilling lives beyond the screen.
In the previous sections, we have address digital wellness and its importance for overall wellbeing. we now provide recommendations for enhancing digital wellness; and why not, creating a more conscious approach to how we use technology and live our lives.
Here are some helpful strategies: Set Boundaries (Setting Screen Time Limits): Establish clear boundaries around device usage by designating specific times for work productivity, studying, socializing, and relaxation. Use features like screen time limits, turn off phone notifications, and app blockers to minimize distractions during focused work sessions.
Practice Digital Detox: Schedule regular breaks from screens to recharge and recalibrate.
Designate device-free zones in living spaces and participate in offline activities such as hobbies, exercise, and social gatherings. Moreover, spend more time outdoors!
Practice Mindfulness: Cultivate mindfulness practices to enhance awareness of digital habits and their impact on well-being. Practice mindful breathing, meditation, or yoga to foster a sense of presence and reduce stress. As a place to start, join the WellU RAM Wellness Program's mindfulness sessions!
Curate Digital Environment: Audit social media feeds and digital content to create a positive and uplifting online environment. Unfollow accounts that trigger negative emotions and seek out platforms that promote inspiration and connection. Yes, social media can be entertaining but it can also harm your mood so stay away from negative sites.
Prioritize Sleep: Maintain consistent sleep schedules and establish bedtime routines conducive to quality rest. Limit screen time before bed and create a calming environment conducive to relaxation and sleep.
Seek Support: Reach out to campus resources such as counseling services for support with managing digital wellness and navigating challenges related to technology use.
Practice Self-Care: Prioritize self-care practices such as healthy nutrition, regular exercise, and adequate hydration to support overall well-being and resilience in the face of digital stressors.
Promote Balance: Strive for balance between academic responsibilities and personal interests by scheduling time for leisure activities, hobbies, and social interactions outside the digital realm.
Become Digitally Literate: Educate yourself about digital literacy and responsible technology use. Simply, create healthy digital habits. Empower yourself to navigate the online world safely and responsibly.
While this is not an exhaustive list of strategies, it is a good starting point in our quest to enhance our digital wellbeing. By implementing these strategies, we can cultivate a healthier relationship with technology and prioritize our well-being despite the demands of our worksites or academic life. Remember, achieving digital wellness is not about eliminating technology entirely but rather fostering a balanced and mindful approach to its use in daily life.
Adapted from Penn State University's 'Less Media, More Social' for Digital Wellness.
Digital Wellbeing: Technology and our daily lives Can We Auto-Correct Humanity? (2024) Social media isn't bad: you're just using it wrong | Eva Amin | TEDxCherryCreekWomen In today's interconnected world, where digital devices and online platforms are found everywhere, maintaining a healthy relationship with technology is paramount for overall wellbeing. Digital wellness encompasses a range of practices aimed at promoting a balanced, intentional and mindful approach to technology usage. Among the myriad recommendations for fostering digital wellness, several key principles emerge as particularly important.
First is the notion of setting boundaries. Establishing clear boundaries around technology use helps individuals maintain control over their digital consumption, while enriching other aspects of life, such as sleep, work, and interpersonal relationships. Thus, setting designated times for device-free activities or creating tech-free zones at home or during leisure time can be effective strategies for enforcing these boundaries.
Another recommendation for optional digital wellness is practicing mindfulness and intentionality in digital interactions. Mindful technology use involves being present and aware of one's actions while engaging with digital devices, rather than mindlessly scrolling or multitasking. Intentionality entails consciously choosing how and why we use technology, aligning our digital activities with our values and priorities. Furthermore, prioritizing digital hygiene is essential for safeguarding mental and emotional well-being. This involves cultivating healthy digital habits, such as limiting screen time, practicing digital detoxes, and regularly auditing and curating online content to minimize exposure to negativity and misinformation.
Finally, fostering meaningful offline connections and activities is vital for achieving digital balance.
Investing time in face-to-face interactions, pursuing hobbies and interests that don't involve screens, and spending time in outdoors are all essential components of a well-rounded lifestyle that prioritizes human connection and real-world experiences over digital distractions.
In conclusion, prioritizing digital wellness requires a multifaceted approach that encompasses setting boundaries, practicing mindfulness, maintaining digital hygiene, and nurturing offline connections. By adopting these recommendations, individuals can cultivate a healthier and more balanced relationship with technology, ultimately leading to improved overall wellbeing in an increasingly digital world.
Carlos Cervantes Carlos is using Smore to create beautiful newsletters

FULL CONTENT:
UA News Center

Home » Tips for Healthy Social Media Habits and Mental Health

Tips for Healthy Social Media Habits and Mental Health

about the AUTHOR

Charlotte Petonic Robbins is the assistant director of student health promotion at The University of Alabama and is a Certified Health Education Specialist.

In recent years, the rise of social media platforms has transformed how we connect with others, share information and express ourselves. While these digital spaces have enriched our lives and increased our connection to others, our social media usage can also negatively affect our mental health.

Despite its positive attributes, social media use comes with challenges, particularly related to our mental health. The constant exposure to carefully curated, idealized representations of others’ lives can lead to social comparison and feelings of inadequacy. The addictive nature of these platforms, driven by algorithms designed to maximize user engagement, can contribute to excessive screen time, disrupted sleep patterns and diminished real-world interactions — factors that are known contributors to mental health issues.

The pressure to maintain a flawless online persona may also contribute to heightened anxiety and stress levels. Cyberbullying, a prevalent issue on social media, further compounds these challenges, exposing individuals to harassment and potentially exacerbating pre-existing mental health conditions. The curated nature of social media content often masks the reality behind the scenes, creating a distorted perception of what constitutes a “normal” or “successful” life.

Signs that social media usage could be affecting your mental health



Tips for using social media

If any of the above applies to you, consider these helpful tips for managing social media usage:

If you need support evaluating your social media usage, students can reach out to the Counseling Center at 205-348-3863, and employees may use the Employee Assistance Program (EAP) with ComPsych.

This story is part of the Mental Well-being series, which features tips and insights on issues related to mental health from experts at The University of Alabama.

SUMMARY:
Protect your mental health. · Take care of your body and mind. · Invest in healthy relationships. · Be intentional about your use of social media,

FULL CONTENT:
![](https://www.facebook.com/tr?id=1129380461262481&ev=PageView&noscript=1)
![GVMA logo](https://gvma.net/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/GVMA-Logo-Dark.svg)
![GVMA logo](https://gvma.net/wp-content/uploads/2022/08/GVMA-Logo-Dark.svg)

# Healthy Social Media Habits

## Social media: How you use it matters.

#### Technology has changed the way people interact with each other. Social media puts other people just keystrokes away. This helps some feel like they have more social support, but it can increase isolation and depression for others.

Researchers are investigating how social media affects mental health. They’re learning that who you meet and what you find online can mean the difference between helpful and harmful effects.

## Use your time wisely.

Logging into social media can lead you in many directions. Actively engaging and connecting with others online can help build your social supports—both online and offline. However, spending many hours passively scrolling through upsetting content can send you spiraling into negative thoughts and feelings.

Increased social media use has been linked to symptoms of depression, anxiety, and stress—but it’s not always clear which comes first: Is more time online causing the symptoms or a result of the symptoms? Depression or anxiety can cause you to isolate yourself. Spending more time online may be a sign that you’re withdrawing from others.

Studies have also found that some online activities can worsen your mental health. Passively watching what others are doing online can make you feel more isolated. You might feel you’re missing out or being left out, or it can make you think that other people have better lives than you.

What you click on then affects what you see next. If you click on things that bother you, you’re likely to be shown more of those things. Repeated scrolling through disturbing content can increase your stress and anxiety.

Teens are especially at risk from the effects of social media. Studies have found links between patterns in teens’ social media use and mental health problems.1 “There has been a growth in social media use, smartphone use, and teens’ lives being online over the last 10 years,” says Dr. Katherine Keyes at Columbia University. Rates of teen depression and suicide also rose over the past 10 years. Researchers have been looking at social media’s role in that increase.2

Keyes’s studies have shown that digital media use alone doesn’t account for these recent rises in depression and suicide. Other factors must also play a role. More research is needed to figure out what those are. What seems most important is how teens are using social media and how their time online is affecting their offline social networks and activities, Keyes says.

In other words, time online takes away from time you could be spending with others, being physically active, or doing a hobby. These are things that help protect your mental health.

## Connect carefully.

You can find countless different people and communities online. “Many meaningful and beneficial connections can be made online,” says Keyes. “This is especially true for teens who have more marginalized identities. Sometimes they can find community and connectivity online that they can’t get in their day-to-day lives.”

However, the digital world can also expose you to harmful health behaviors. Excessive drinking, substance use, and eating disorders are sometimes misleadingly shown as what everyone’s doing or wants to do.3

People also see ads about tobacco use, cannabis use, and drinking online. Dr. Patricia Cavazos-Rehg at Washington University in St. Louis studies the effects of ads on teen substance use. Her research has shown that even passively viewing tobacco content online increased the likelihood of using tobacco products.4 Keyes found similar trends for alcohol and cannabis. “My concern is that social media can make substance use behaviors seem normal,” Cavazos-Rehg explains. That can affect both teens and adults.

“We’ve seen a lot of messages online about ‘wine-mom’ culture that link alcohol use with ‘mommy needs a break at the end of the day,'” Keyes says.5 “These messages link alcohol with positive self-care.” However, using alcohol to manage stress is not a healthy coping strategy. In recent years, women have had a higher increase in alcohol use than men.6

Cavazos-Rehg is researching ways to deliver information about the risks of substance use on social media. She’s also looking at how to get quality treatment information to people talking about mental health issues and substance use online.7

## Seek out help.

Social media can be a tool to improve your mental health. You can search for health information, hear about others’ experiences, or find treatment options. “We have found that social media can be very helpful for people who are feeling stigmatized about in-person recovery,” Cavazos-Rehg says. “Plus, social media can help those who are curious or ready to engage in treatment but want advice from their online peers first.”

Her team looked at what prevents people with symptoms of depression from seeking treatment. They found that many people worry about being stigmatized. Others have trouble accessing or paying for treatment. Her team is looking for ways to reduce those barriers through social media.7 They’ve created tools to identify social media posts that may indicate someone needs treatment for an eating disorder.8 They also created a treatment app for teens with eating disorders. The team is working to reach teens in need of treatment through online ads as well.

“There is a lot of support for recovery and for mental health that individuals can get off of social media,” says Cavazos-Rehg. “But there’s often misinformation that can spread as well.” Find tips for evaluating online health information at <https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/finding-and-evaluating-online-resources> and <https://www.nia.nih.gov/health/online-health-information-it-reliable>.

Remember, you don’t need to struggle with mental health problems alone. “There’s a common misconception that we can handle our mental health problems on our own, and that they’re not severe enough to warrant medical care,” Cavazos-Rehg says. “But that’s a misconception.” Don’t hesitate to reach out to a health care provider or mental health professional.

## Protect your mental health.

## Further Reading

[“Is Social Media Making Us Lonelier?” U.S. National Institutes of Health (NIH) Director’s Blog](https://directorsblog.nih.gov/2017/03/14/is-social-media-making-us-lonelier/)

## References

Wein, H. (Ed.). (2022, September). Healthy social media habits: How you use it matters. *NIH News in Health*. Bethesda, MD: U.S. National Institutes of Health (NIH). Retrieved September 8, 2022, from https://newsinhealth.nih.gov

enriched_points.expanded_content: Encourage practices such as limiting screen time, engaging in digital detoxes, and curating online content to foster a healthier relationship with technology and enhance overall well-being.

enriched_points.point_title: Engage in Open Discussions about Mental Health
enriched_points.expanded_content: Facilitate conversations surrounding mental health challenges and experiences, creating a supportive environment that reduces stigma and promotes understanding among peers and communities.
Leverage digital tools and resources intentionally, prioritizing credible information and supportive platforms to enhance mental health awareness and access to professional help.

enriched_points.point_title: Support Initiatives for Mental Health Awareness
enriched_points.expanded_content: Get involved in campaigns and programs dedicated to mental health education and awareness, helping to foster a culture of support and understanding within communities.
Promote active participation in local mental health events and initiatives, strengthening community bonds and fostering connections that support mental well-being.
